content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers 35 (NUM_035)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 35:1 -- "in the plains" vs. "on the plains"; "[at] Jericho" vs. "opposite Jericho" vs. "at Jericho"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And YHWH spoke to Moses in the plains of Moab by the Jordan [at] Jericho, saying," |
| LOGOS | "And YHWH spoke to Moses in the plains of Moab by the Jordan, opposite Jericho, saying," |
| PNEUMA | "And YHWH spoke to Moses on the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho, saying," |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And YHWH spoke to Moses in the plains of Moab by the Jordan, opposite Jericho, saying," -- (1) "in the plains" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "on the plains" (PNEUMA) for be'arvot; the Hebrew preposition be- conveys "in" rather than "on" for a geographical region. (2) "opposite Jericho" (LOGOS) is adopted over "[at] Jericho" (EMET, with bracketed preposition) and "at Jericho" (PNEUMA). The Hebrew yericho here functions appositionally to describe the location across the Jordan from Jericho, and "opposite" captures the Transjordanian setting; the Israelites are not yet in Jericho. EMET's brackets around "[at]" indicate the preposition is supplied, and LOGOS's "opposite" better conveys the geographical reality. (3) EMET omits the comma before the Jericho reference; the comma (LOGOS) is adopted for clarity.

---

### 35:2 -- "that they give" vs. "to give"; sentence structure
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Command the sons of Israel that they give to the Levites from the inheritance of their possession cities to dwell in; and pastureland for the cities all around them you shall give to the Levites." |
| LOGOS | "Command the sons of Israel that they give to the Levites, from the inheritance of their possession, cities to dwell in; and pastureland for the cities all around them you shall give to the Levites." |
| PNEUMA | "Command the sons of Israel to give to the Levites, from the inheritance they possess, cities in which to dwell. And you shall give to the Levites pasturelands surrounding the cities." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Command the sons of Israel that they give to the Levites from the inheritance of their possession cities to dwell in; and pastureland for the cities all around them you shall give to the Levites." -- (1) "that they give" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "to give" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew veyatenu uses the conjunctive perfect, conveying a command relayed through a subordinate clause ("that they give"), not a bare infinitive. (2) "from the inheritance of their possession" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "from the inheritance they possess" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew me-nachalat achuzatam uses a construct chain (inheritance of their possession), not a relative clause. (3) "cities to dwell in" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "cities in which to dwell" (PNEUMA), which is more formally English but adds a relative pronoun not in the Hebrew. (4) PNEUMA restructures the second clause, placing "pasturelands" as the object before the verb and using "surrounding" instead of "all around them"; the EMET/LOGOS word order ("and pastureland for the cities all around them you shall give to the Levites") better follows the Hebrew syntax. (5) "pastureland" (singular, EMET) is adopted, as the Hebrew migrash here is singular in construct; PNEUMA uses the plural "pasturelands." (6) LOGOS adds commas setting off "from the inheritance of their possession" as a parenthetical; EMET's unpunctuated flow is adopted as closer to the Hebrew continuous clause.

---

### 35:3 -- "living creatures" vs. "animals"; "goods" vs. "livestock"; "cattle" vs. "livestock"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the cities shall be for them to dwell in, and their pasturelands shall be for their livestock and for their goods and for all their living creatures." |
| LOGOS | "And the cities shall be for them to dwell in, and their pasturelands shall be for their livestock and for their goods and for all their animals." |
| PNEUMA | "And the cities shall be theirs to dwell in, and their pasturelands shall be for their cattle and for their livestock and for all their animals." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the cities shall be for them to dwell in, and their pasturelands shall be for their livestock and for their goods and for all their animals." -- (1) "for them to dwell in" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "theirs to dwell in" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew lahem lashevet preserves the dative construction "for them to dwell." (2) "livestock" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted for behemtam over "cattle" (PNEUMA); behemah is a general term for domesticated animals, not limited to bovines. (3) "goods" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted for rekusham over "livestock" (PNEUMA); rekush refers to property/possessions/goods, not animals -- PNEUMA's rendering creates redundancy with the first term. (4) "animals" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "living creatures" (EMET) for chayyatam; while chayyah can mean "living creature," in this pastoral context "animals" is the more natural rendering and carries the same semantic range. The three Hebrew terms (behemah, rekush, chayyah) represent a comprehensive enumeration: domesticated animals, movable property, and all living creatures generally.

---

### 35:4 -- "[shall extend]" (bracketed) vs. "shall extend" (unbracketed)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the pasturelands of the cities which you shall give to the Levites [shall extend] from the wall of the city and outward one thousand cubits all around." |
| LOGOS | "And the pasturelands of the cities which you shall give to the Levites shall extend from the wall of the city outward one thousand cubits all around." |
| PNEUMA | "And the pasturelands of the cities that you give to the Levites shall extend from the wall of the city outward one thousand cubits all around." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the pasturelands of the cities which you shall give to the Levites shall extend from the wall of the city outward one thousand cubits all around." -- (1) EMET brackets "[shall extend]" as a supplied verb; LOGOS and PNEUMA include it without brackets. The verb is implied in the Hebrew verbless clause and reads naturally unbracketed in English. (2) EMET includes "and outward" where LOGOS/PNEUMA have simply "outward"; the Hebrew vachutsa begins with a waw, but "outward" alone suffices since it modifies the directional measurement and the "and" is better absorbed. (3) "which you shall give" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "that you give" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew asher titenu uses the imperfect, better rendered with "shall give" to preserve the future/prescriptive sense.

---

### 35:5 -- "eastern side" vs. "east side"; "in the middle" vs. "at the center"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "...on the eastern side two thousand cubits, and on the southern side two thousand cubits, and on the western side two thousand cubits, and on the northern side two thousand cubits, with the city in the middle. This shall be to them the pasturelands of the cities." |
| LOGOS | "...on the east side two thousand cubits, and on the south side two thousand cubits, and on the west side two thousand cubits, and on the north side two thousand cubits, with the city in the middle. This shall be to them the pasturelands of the cities." |
| PNEUMA | "...on the eastern side two thousand cubits, and on the southern side two thousand cubits, and on the western side two thousand cubits, and on the northern side two thousand cubits -- with the city at the center. This shall be for them the pasturelands of the cities." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "...on the eastern side two thousand cubits, and on the southern side two thousand cubits, and on the western side two thousand cubits, and on the northern side two thousand cubits, with the city in the middle. This shall be to them the pasturelands of the cities." -- (1) "eastern/southern/western/northern" (EMET/PNEUMA) is adopted over "east/south/west/north" (LOGOS); the Hebrew pe'at negev, pe'at yam, etc. use construct forms that are better rendered with adjective forms in English. (2) "in the middle" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "at the center" (PNEUMA) for batavekh; the Hebrew tavekh means "midst/middle," and "middle" is the more literal rendering. (3) PNEUMA uses an em-dash before "with the city"; comma (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted. (4) "to them" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "for them" (PNEUMA) for lahem; while both prepositions are possible, "to them" preserves the dative of possession.

---

### 35:6 -- "[shall be]" vs. "shall be"; "besides them" vs. "in addition to them"; "for the manslayer" vs. "for the one who kills someone"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the cities which you shall give to the Levites [shall be] the six cities of refuge which you shall give for the manslayer to flee there, and besides them you shall give forty-two cities." |
| LOGOS | "And the cities which you shall give to the Levites shall be the six cities of refuge, which you shall give for the manslayer to flee there; and in addition to them you shall give forty-two cities." |
| PNEUMA | "And among the cities that you give to the Levites shall be the six cities of refuge, which you shall designate for the one who kills someone to flee there. And in addition to them you shall give forty-two cities." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the cities which you shall give to the Levites shall be the six cities of refuge, which you shall give for the manslayer to flee there; and in addition to them you shall give forty-two cities." -- (1) "shall be" without brackets (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over EMET's bracketed "[shall be]." (2) "in addition to them" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "besides them" (EMET) for va'aleihem; both are valid, but "in addition to" is clearer in modern English. (3) "for the manslayer" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "for the one who kills someone" (PNEUMA); rotseach is a single Hebrew term best rendered with a single English word, and "manslayer" is the established convention. (4) "give" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "designate" (PNEUMA) for titenu; the Hebrew verb is natan (give), not a verb meaning designate. (5) PNEUMA restructures with "among the cities" and breaks into two sentences; LOGOS's structure with semicolon is adopted as preserving the Hebrew continuous clause. (6) LOGOS's comma before "which you shall give" is adopted, adding appropriate clause separation.

---

### 35:8 -- "from the many/few" vs. "from the larger/smaller tribe"; "make many/few" vs. "give more/fewer" vs. "take more/fewer"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "...from the many you shall make many, and from the few you shall make few; each according to his inheritance which he inherits shall give from his cities to the Levites." |
| LOGOS | "...from the larger tribe you shall give more, and from the smaller tribe you shall give fewer. Each one according to his inheritance which he inherits shall give of his cities to the Levites." |
| PNEUMA | "...from the larger tribe you shall take more, and from the smaller tribe you shall take fewer. Each tribe shall give from its cities to the Levites in proportion to the inheritance it receives." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "...from the many you shall give more, and from the few you shall give fewer. Each one according to his inheritance which he inherits shall give of his cities to the Levites." -- (1) "from the many ... from the few" (EMET) is closer to the Hebrew me'et harav ... me'et hame'at, which uses substantive adjectives ("the many," "the few") without explicitly adding "tribe." LOGOS/PNEUMA add "tribe" interpretively. However, EMET's "make many/make few" for tarbu/tam'itu is overly literal for the Hiphil forms, which mean "increase/decrease" in terms of giving; "give more/give fewer" (LOGOS) better captures the sense. (2) "take" (PNEUMA) is rejected; the Hebrew yarbu/yam'itu are Hiphil forms meaning "to cause to be many/few" (i.e., give more/fewer), not "take." (3) "Each one according to his inheritance which he inherits" (LOGOS) is adopted over EMET's "each according to his inheritance" (missing "one") and PNEUMA's "each tribe ... in proportion to the inheritance it receives" (interpretive paraphrase). (4) "give of his cities" (LOGOS) is adopted over "give from his cities" (EMET/PNEUMA); the Hebrew me'arav is partitive, and "of" better conveys this. (5) PNEUMA's "in proportion to the inheritance it receives" is an interpretive expansion of the Hebrew ish kefi nachalato; the literal rendering is adopted.

---

### 35:11 -- "then you shall select" vs. "you shall select"; sentence structure
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "then you shall select for yourselves cities -- cities of refuge they shall be for you -- and the manslayer who strikes a person unintentionally shall flee there." |
| LOGOS | "then you shall select for yourselves cities to be cities of refuge for you, that the manslayer who strikes a person unintentionally may flee there." |
| PNEUMA | "you shall select cities for yourselves -- cities of refuge they shall be for you -- so that one who kills a person unintentionally may flee there." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "then you shall select for yourselves cities -- cities of refuge they shall be for you -- and the manslayer who strikes a person unintentionally shall flee there." -- (1) "then" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted; PNEUMA omits it, but the Hebrew vehiqritem follows the temporal clause of verse 10 and "then" properly marks this sequential relationship. (2) "for yourselves cities" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "cities for yourselves" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew lakhem arim places the dative before the object. (3) "cities of refuge they shall be for you" set off with dashes (EMET/PNEUMA) preserves the Hebrew appositional structure better than LOGOS's "cities to be cities of refuge for you," which smooths it into a purpose clause. (4) "the manslayer who strikes" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "one who kills" (PNEUMA); rotseach is the established term "manslayer," and the Hebrew uses nakeh (strikes), not a generic "kills." (5) "shall flee" (EMET) is adopted over "may flee" (LOGOS/PNEUMA); the Hebrew lanus sham conveys the provision of refuge as a right/command, and "shall" better suits the prescriptive legal context.

---

### 35:12 -- "the avenger" vs. "the blood-avenger"; "and the manslayer" vs. "so that the manslayer" vs. "so that the one who kills"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the cities shall be for you a refuge from the avenger, and the manslayer shall not die until he stands before the congregation for judgment." |
| LOGOS | "And the cities shall be for you a refuge from the avenger, so that the manslayer shall not die until he stands before the congregation for judgment." |
| PNEUMA | "And the cities shall serve you as refuge from the blood-avenger, so that the one who kills shall not die until he stands before the congregation for judgment." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the cities shall be for you a refuge from the avenger, so that the manslayer shall not die until he stands before the congregation for judgment." -- (1) "the avenger" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "the blood-avenger" (PNEUMA) for hago'el; in this verse the Hebrew has only go'el without haddam ("the blood"), so the shorter form is correct. (2) "so that" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "and" (EMET); the Hebrew velo yamut expresses purpose/result, and "so that" better conveys this than a bare conjunction. (3) "the manslayer" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "the one who kills" (PNEUMA) for harotseach. (4) "shall be for you a refuge" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "shall serve you as refuge" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew vehayu lakhem is "shall be for you," not "shall serve."

---

### 35:14 -- "from across the Jordan" vs. "beyond the Jordan" vs. "on the other side of the Jordan"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Three of the cities you shall give from across the Jordan, and three of the cities you shall give in the land of Canaan; cities of refuge they shall be." |
| LOGOS | "Three cities you shall give beyond the Jordan, and three cities you shall give in the land of Canaan; cities of refuge shall they be." |
| PNEUMA | "Three of the cities you shall give on the other side of the Jordan, and three of the cities you shall give in the land of Canaan. Cities of refuge they shall be." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Three of the cities you shall give beyond the Jordan, and three of the cities you shall give in the land of Canaan; cities of refuge they shall be." -- (1) "beyond the Jordan" (LOGOS) is adopted over "from across the Jordan" (EMET) and "on the other side of the Jordan" (PNEUMA) for me'ever layarden; "beyond" is the standard rendering of ever and is concise. (2) "Three of the cities" (EMET/PNEUMA) is adopted over "Three cities" (LOGOS); the Hebrew et shelosh he'arim uses the definite article, indicating "three of the [aforementioned] cities." (3) "cities of refuge they shall be" (EMET/PNEUMA) is adopted over "cities of refuge shall they be" (LOGOS); the former better preserves the Hebrew word order where arei miqlat is fronted as a predicate nominative. (4) PNEUMA breaks into two sentences with a period; the semicolon (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew clause connection.

---

### 35:15 -- "a refuge" vs. "for refuge" vs. "for a refuge"; "striking" vs. "who strikes" vs. "who kills"; "resident" vs. "resident alien"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For the sons of Israel and for the sojourner and for the resident among them, these six cities shall be for a refuge, for anyone striking a person unintentionally to flee there." |
| LOGOS | "For the sons of Israel and for the sojourner and for the resident among them, these six cities shall be for refuge, for anyone who strikes a person unintentionally to flee there." |
| PNEUMA | "For the sons of Israel and for the sojourner and for the resident alien among them, these six cities shall be for refuge, so that anyone who kills a person unintentionally may flee there." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "For the sons of Israel and for the sojourner and for the resident among them, these six cities shall be for refuge, for anyone who strikes a person unintentionally to flee there." -- (1) "for refuge" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "for a refuge" (EMET); the Hebrew lemiqlat lacks the article, and the abstract "for refuge" is more natural. (2) "who strikes" (LOGOS) is adopted over "striking" (EMET, participial) and "who kills" (PNEUMA, interpretive); the Hebrew makkeh is a participle meaning "the one who strikes," and the relative clause "who strikes" conveys this clearly while "kills" imports a meaning beyond the Hebrew nakkeh. (3) "the resident" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "the resident alien" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew toshav means "resident/settler" without an explicit "alien" qualifier. (4) PNEUMA restructures the final clause with "so that ... may flee"; "for anyone who strikes ... to flee there" (LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew infinitive construction lenus shammah.

---

### 35:16 -- sentence structure divergence
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And if with an instrument of iron he struck him and he died, he [is] a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death." |
| LOGOS | "And if he struck him with an instrument of iron so that he died, he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death." |
| PNEUMA | "But if he strikes him with an instrument of iron and he dies -- he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And if with an instrument of iron he struck him and he died, he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death." -- (1) "And if" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "But if" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew ve'im uses the conjunctive waw, not an adversative. (2) "with an instrument of iron he struck him" (EMET) preserves the Hebrew word order where the prepositional phrase precedes the verb (bikhli varzel hikkahu); LOGOS moves it after the verb. The fronted instrument phrase is adopted as it emphasizes the weapon in this legal categorization. (3) "and he died" (EMET) is adopted over "so that he died" (LOGOS) and "and he dies" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew vayyamot is a simple waw-consecutive without purposive force, and the past tense matches the casuistic legal form. (4) PNEUMA uses present tense ("strikes," "dies") throughout; the Hebrew uses perfect/waw-consecutive forms that are standard for legal casuistic formulation and are best rendered in English past tense. (5) EMET's bracketed "[is]" is removed; the copula reads naturally without brackets. (6) PNEUMA uses em-dash and period, breaking into two sentences; the semicolon preserves the unified legal formula.

---

### 35:17 -- "a stone [in the] hand" vs. "a stone in hand" vs. "a stone in hand large enough to kill"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And if with a stone [in the] hand by which one could die he struck him and he died, he [is] a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death." |
| LOGOS | "And if he struck him with a stone in hand by which one could die, and he died, he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death." |
| PNEUMA | "And if he strikes him with a stone in hand large enough to kill, and he dies -- he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And if with a stone in hand by which one could die he struck him and he died, he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death." -- (1) The fronted prepositional phrase "with a stone in hand by which one could die" (following EMET's word order) is adopted to match the Hebrew syntax where the instrument precedes the verb. (2) "by which one could die" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "large enough to kill" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew asher yamut bah is literally "by which he could die," and PNEUMA's paraphrase, while capturing the sense, departs from the Hebrew construction. (3) EMET's brackets around "[in the]" are removed. (4) Tense and punctuation decisions follow the same principles as verse 16.

---

### 35:18 -- "wooden instrument" vs. "wooden weapon"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Or [if] with a wooden instrument [in the] hand by which one could die he struck him and he died, he [is] a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death." |
| LOGOS | "Or if he struck him with a wooden instrument in hand by which one could die, and he died, he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death." |
| PNEUMA | "Or if he strikes him with a wooden weapon in hand large enough to kill, and he dies -- he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Or if with a wooden instrument in hand by which one could die he struck him and he died, he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death." -- (1) "instrument" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "weapon" (PNEUMA) for kli etz; kli is a general term meaning "vessel, instrument, utensil" -- "weapon" imports a martial connotation not inherent in the Hebrew. (2) EMET's brackets around "[if]" and "[in the]" are removed. (3) Word order, tense, and punctuation follow the same principles as verses 16-17.

---

### 35:19 -- "avenger of blood" vs. "blood-avenger"; "put to death the murderer" vs. "put the murderer to death"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "The avenger of blood -- he himself shall put to death the murderer; when he encounters him, he shall put him to death." |
| LOGOS | "The avenger of blood -- he himself shall put the murderer to death; when he encounters him, he shall put him to death." |
| PNEUMA | "The blood-avenger himself shall put the murderer to death. When he encounters him, he shall put him to death." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "The avenger of blood -- he himself shall put the murderer to death; when he encounters him, he shall put him to death." -- (1) "The avenger of blood" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "The blood-avenger" (PNEUMA) for go'el haddam; the construct phrase in Hebrew has the nomen regens followed by the genitive, and "avenger of blood" preserves this structure. (2) "he himself shall put the murderer to death" (LOGOS) is adopted over "he himself shall put to death the murderer" (EMET); English word order places the object before the complement more naturally. (3) The em-dash after "blood" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew casus pendens (topicalized subject) better than PNEUMA's direct predication. (4) PNEUMA breaks into two sentences; the semicolon (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the unified legal formula.

---

### 35:20 -- "threw at him [something]" vs. "hurled something at him" vs. "hurls something at him"; "with intent" vs. "with malicious intent"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And if in hatred he pushed him, or threw at him [something] with intent, and he died," |
| LOGOS | "And if he pushed him out of hatred, or hurled something at him with intent, and he died," |
| PNEUMA | "And if out of hatred he pushes him, or hurls something at him with malicious intent and he dies," |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And if in hatred he pushed him, or hurled at him something with intent, and he died," -- (1) "in hatred" (EMET) is adopted over "out of hatred" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for besin'ah; the Hebrew uses the preposition be- ("in"), not min ("out of/from"). (2) "hurled" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "threw" (EMET) for hishlikh; both are valid, but "hurled" better conveys the deliberate force implied. (3) "with intent" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "with malicious intent" (PNEUMA) for bitsediyyah; the Hebrew tsediyyah means "lying in wait/premeditation" -- "malicious" is an interpretive addition, as the intent is already established by the context of "in hatred." (4) EMET's "[something]" in brackets is adopted without brackets as "something," since the object is implied in the Hebrew but necessary in English. (5) Past tense (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over present tense (PNEUMA) for the casuistic legal form.

---

### 35:21 -- clause ordering
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "or in enmity he struck him with his hand and he died, the one who struck [him] shall surely be put to death -- he [is] a murderer; the avenger of blood shall put to death the murderer when he encounters him." |
| LOGOS | "or in enmity struck him with his hand, and he died, the one who struck him shall surely be put to death; he is a murderer. The avenger of blood shall put the murderer to death when he encounters him." |
| PNEUMA | "or in enmity strikes him with his hand and he dies -- the one who struck him shall surely be put to death; he is a murderer. The blood-avenger shall put the murderer to death when he encounters him." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "or in enmity he struck him with his hand and he died, the one who struck him shall surely be put to death; he is a murderer. The avenger of blood shall put the murderer to death when he encounters him." -- (1) "he struck" (EMET) is adopted over the subjectless "struck" (LOGOS) for clarity, and over present tense "strikes" (PNEUMA). (2) "the one who struck him" without brackets (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over EMET's "[him]" bracket. (3) "he is a murderer" placed after "shall surely be put to death" follows LOGOS/PNEUMA, which matches the Hebrew clause order better than EMET's dash-separated structure. (4) "The avenger of blood" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "The blood-avenger" (PNEUMA). (5) A period before the avenger clause (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted; the sentence is long enough to warrant a break at this natural division.

---

### 35:22 -- "threw" vs. "hurled" vs. "throws"; "with intent" vs. "with malicious intent"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But if suddenly, without enmity, he pushed him, or threw upon him any instrument without intent," |
| LOGOS | "But if suddenly, without enmity, he pushed him, or hurled upon him any instrument without intent," |
| PNEUMA | "But if suddenly, without enmity, he pushes him, or throws any object at him without malicious intent," |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "But if suddenly, without enmity, he pushed him, or hurled upon him any instrument without intent," -- (1) "hurled" (LOGOS) is adopted over "threw" (EMET) and "throws" (PNEUMA) for consistency with verse 20. (2) "upon him any instrument" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "any object at him" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew alav kol-kli uses the preposition al ("upon") and kli ("instrument/vessel"), not "object." (3) "without intent" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "without malicious intent" (PNEUMA) for belo tsediyyah, as in verse 20. (4) Past tense adopted over PNEUMA's present tense.

---

### 35:23 -- "and he caused [it] to fall" vs. "and it fell" vs. "and it falls"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "or [with] any stone by which one could die, without seeing [him], and he caused [it] to fall upon him and he died, and he [was] not his enemy and [was] not seeking his harm," |
| LOGOS | "or with any stone by which one could die, without seeing him, and it fell upon him, and he died, and he was not his enemy and was not seeking his harm," |
| PNEUMA | "or with any stone large enough to kill, without seeing him, and it falls upon him and he dies -- and he was not his enemy, and he did not seek his harm --" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "or with any stone by which one could die, without seeing him, and it fell upon him and he died, and he was not his enemy and was not seeking his harm," -- (1) "by which one could die" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "large enough to kill" (PNEUMA), as in verse 17. (2) "and it fell upon him" (LOGOS) is adopted over "and he caused [it] to fall upon him" (EMET); while the Hebrew vayyappel can be read as Hiphil causative, the Qal intransitive reading ("it fell") is more natural in this accidental context and aligns with the Vaticanus. (3) "was not seeking" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "did not seek" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew mevaqesh is a participle, expressing an ongoing state ("was not seeking") rather than a punctiliar action ("did not seek"). (4) EMET's multiple brackets are removed. (5) PNEUMA's em-dashes at the end depart from the Hebrew clause structure; the comma continuation (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted.

---

### 35:24 -- "the one who struck" vs. "the striker"; "judgments" vs. "ordinances"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "then the congregation shall judge between the one who struck and the avenger of blood according to these judgments." |
| LOGOS | "then the congregation shall judge between the striker and the avenger of blood according to these ordinances." |
| PNEUMA | "then the congregation shall judge between the one who struck and the blood-avenger according to these ordinances." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "then the congregation shall judge between the one who struck and the avenger of blood according to these judgments." -- (1) "the one who struck" (EMET/PNEUMA) is adopted over "the striker" (LOGOS) for hammakkeh; while "striker" is a valid rendering of the participle, "the one who struck" is more transparent in English legal language. (2) "the avenger of blood" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "the blood-avenger" (PNEUMA). (3) "judgments" (EMET) is adopted over "ordinances" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for hammishpatim; mishpat in its primary sense means "judgment/justice," and in this legal context where the congregation is performing a judicial act, "judgments" is the most fitting rendering. "Ordinances" shifts the sense toward codified law, whereas the Hebrew here refers to the specific judicial criteria outlined in the preceding verses.

---

### 35:25 -- "deliver" vs. "rescue"; "where he fled" vs. "to which he had fled"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the congregation shall deliver the manslayer from the hand of the avenger of blood, and the congregation shall return him to his city of refuge where he fled, and he shall dwell in it until the death of the high priest who was anointed with the holy oil." |
| LOGOS | "And the congregation shall deliver the manslayer from the hand of the avenger of blood, and the congregation shall return him to his city of refuge to which he had fled; and he shall dwell in it until the death of the high priest who was anointed with the holy oil." |
| PNEUMA | "And the congregation shall rescue the one who killed from the hand of the blood-avenger, and the congregation shall return him to his city of refuge where he had fled. And he shall dwell in it until the death of the high priest who was anointed with the holy oil." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the congregation shall deliver the manslayer from the hand of the avenger of blood, and the congregation shall return him to his city of refuge to which he had fled; and he shall dwell in it until the death of the high priest who was anointed with the holy oil." -- (1) "deliver" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "rescue" (PNEUMA) for hitsilu; while both are valid for the Hiphil of natsal, "deliver" is the more standard rendering in biblical legal contexts and avoids the dramatic overtone of "rescue." (2) "the manslayer" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "the one who killed" (PNEUMA). (3) "to which he had fled" (LOGOS) is adopted over "where he fled" (EMET) and "where he had fled" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew asher nas shammah uses asher + shammah (relative + "there"), and "to which he had fled" is more precise, with the pluperfect "had fled" correctly indicating the flight preceded the trial. (4) "the avenger of blood" (EMET/LOGOS) over "the blood-avenger" (PNEUMA). (5) Semicolon before "and he shall dwell" (LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's period.

---

### 35:26 -- "border" vs. "boundary"; "where he flees" vs. "to which he fled"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But if the manslayer shall indeed go out beyond the border of his city of refuge where he flees," |
| LOGOS | "But if the manslayer shall indeed go out beyond the boundary of his city of refuge to which he fled," |
| PNEUMA | "But if the one who killed ever goes beyond the boundary of his city of refuge where he fled," |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "But if the manslayer shall indeed go out beyond the border of his city of refuge to which he fled," -- (1) "border" (EMET) is adopted over "boundary" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for gevul, consistent with project conventions established in Numbers 34. (2) "the manslayer" (EMET/LOGOS) over "the one who killed" (PNEUMA). (3) "shall indeed go out" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the infinitive absolute yatso yetse ("going out, he goes out") with the doubling convention; "ever goes" (PNEUMA) captures the emphasis but loses the formal doubling pattern. (4) "to which he fled" (LOGOS) is adopted over "where he flees" (EMET) and "where he fled" (PNEUMA); EMET's present tense "flees" does not match the Hebrew nas (perfect), and LOGOS's "to which" is more precise for the relative construction.

---

### 35:27 -- "blood-guilt" vs. "bloodguilt"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "...and the avenger of blood kills the manslayer -- there is no blood-guilt upon him." |
| LOGOS | "...and the avenger of blood kills the manslayer, there is no bloodguilt upon him." |
| PNEUMA | "...and the blood-avenger kills the one who killed -- there is no bloodguilt upon him." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "...and the avenger of blood kills the manslayer -- there is no bloodguilt upon him." -- (1) "bloodguilt" (LOGOS/PNEUMA, unhyphenated) is adopted over "blood-guilt" (EMET, hyphenated); the compound has become standard in English biblical scholarship as one word. (2) The em-dash (EMET/PNEUMA) is adopted over LOGOS's comma before "there is no bloodguilt"; the dash better marks the abrupt legal declaration. (3) "the avenger of blood" and "the manslayer" are adopted per standard conventions.

---

### 35:28 -- "shall dwell" vs. "must dwell"; "shall return" vs. "may return"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For in his city of refuge he shall dwell until the death of the high priest, and after the death of the high priest the manslayer shall return to the land of his possession." |
| LOGOS | "For in his city of refuge he shall dwell until the death of the high priest, and after the death of the high priest the manslayer may return to the land of his possession." |
| PNEUMA | "For in his city of refuge he must dwell until the death of the high priest. And after the death of the high priest, the one who killed may return to the land of his possession." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "For in his city of refuge he shall dwell until the death of the high priest, and after the death of the high priest the manslayer shall return to the land of his possession." -- (1) "shall dwell" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "must dwell" (PNEUMA); "shall" is the standard modal for legal prescription in this translation. (2) "shall return" (EMET) is adopted over "may return" (LOGOS/PNEUMA); the Hebrew yashuv is an imperfect indicating what will happen after the priest's death, not merely permission. The return is the expected and prescribed outcome. (3) "the manslayer" (EMET/LOGOS) over "the one who killed" (PNEUMA). (4) Single sentence with comma continuation (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's two-sentence break.

---

### 35:30 -- "by the mouth of witnesses" vs. "according to the mouth of witnesses" vs. "on the testimony of witnesses"; "to die" vs. "to put him to death"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Anyone who strikes a person -- by [the] mouth of witnesses shall the murderer be put to death, but one witness shall not testify against a person to die." |
| LOGOS | "Anyone who strikes a person -- according to the mouth of witnesses shall the murderer be put to death; but one witness shall not testify against a person to put him to death." |
| PNEUMA | "Anyone who kills a person -- on the testimony of witnesses shall the murderer be put to death. But a single witness shall not testify against a person to put him to death." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Anyone who strikes a person -- by the mouth of witnesses shall the murderer be put to death; but one witness shall not testify against a person to put him to death." -- (1) "by the mouth of witnesses" is adopted, combining EMET's "by" with the unbracketed article; the Hebrew lefi is literally "according to the mouth of," and "by the mouth" is a well-established biblical idiom in English. LOGOS's "according to the mouth" is more literal but cumbersome; PNEUMA's "on the testimony" loses the concrete Hebrew metaphor of "mouth." (2) "strikes" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "kills" (PNEUMA) for makkeh nefesh; the Hebrew uses the general "strike a person." (3) "to put him to death" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "to die" (EMET) for lamut; while lamut literally means "to die," the context is juridical sentencing and "to put him to death" conveys the legal sense more clearly. (4) "one witness" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "a single witness" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew ed echad is literally "witness one" -- "one witness" is sufficiently clear. (5) Semicolon before "but" (LOGOS) is adopted.

---

### 35:31 -- "who [is] guilty of death" vs. "who is guilty of death" vs. "who is guilty and deserves to die"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And you shall not take a ransom for the life of a murderer who [is] guilty of death, for he shall surely be put to death." |
| LOGOS | "And you shall not take a ransom for the life of a murderer who is guilty of death, for he shall surely be put to death." |
| PNEUMA | "And you shall accept no ransom for the life of a murderer who is guilty and deserves to die; he shall surely be put to death." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And you shall not take a ransom for the life of a murderer who is guilty of death, for he shall surely be put to death." -- (1) "take" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "accept" (PNEUMA) for tiqchu; the Hebrew laqach means "take," and "accept" introduces a nuance of willingness not in the Hebrew. (2) "who is guilty of death" (LOGOS) without brackets is adopted over EMET's "[is]." (3) "who is guilty of death" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "who is guilty and deserves to die" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew asher hu rasha lamut means "who is guilty/wicked to die" -- PNEUMA's expansion "and deserves to die" is interpretive. (4) "for he shall surely be put to death" (EMET/LOGOS) with "for" is adopted over PNEUMA's semicolon; the Hebrew ki introduces a causal clause.

---

### 35:32 -- "for him to flee" vs. "for him who has fled" vs. "for one who has fled"; "the priest" vs. "the high priest"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And you shall not take a ransom for him to flee to his city of refuge, to return to dwell in the land before the death of the priest." |
| LOGOS | "And you shall not take a ransom for him who has fled to his city of refuge, to return to dwell in the land before the death of the priest." |
| PNEUMA | "And you shall accept no ransom for one who has fled to his city of refuge, allowing him to return to dwell in the land before the death of the high priest." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And you shall not take a ransom for him who has fled to his city of refuge, to return to dwell in the land before the death of the priest." -- (1) "take" (EMET/LOGOS) over "accept" (PNEUMA), as in verse 31. (2) "for him who has fled" (LOGOS) is adopted over "for him to flee" (EMET) and "for one who has fled" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew lanus refers to one who has already fled (the manslayer in refuge), not the act of fleeing. LOGOS's relative clause clarifies this. (3) "the priest" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "the high priest" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew in this verse reads hakkohen without haggadol ("the great"). PNEUMA adds "high" from context (vv. 25, 28), but the Hebrew here uses the shorter form and this distinction should be preserved. (4) "to return to dwell" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "allowing him to return to dwell" (PNEUMA); the Hebrew lashuv lashevet uses infinitives without a participial "allowing."

---

### 35:33 -- "in which you [are]" vs. "in which you are" vs. "in which you live"; "the blood" vs. "blood"; sentence structure
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And you shall not pollute the land in which you [are], for the blood -- it pollutes the land, and for the land no atonement can be made for the blood which is shed in it except by the blood of the one who shed it." |
| LOGOS | "And you shall not pollute the land in which you are, for blood -- it pollutes the land, and no atonement can be made for the land for the blood that is shed in it, except by the blood of the one who shed it." |
| PNEUMA | "And you shall not pollute the land in which you live, for blood -- it pollutes the land. And no atonement can be made for the land for the blood that is shed in it, except by the blood of the one who shed it." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And you shall not pollute the land in which you are, for blood -- it pollutes the land, and no atonement can be made for the land for the blood that is shed in it, except by the blood of the one who shed it." -- (1) "in which you are" (LOGOS) without brackets is adopted over EMET's "[are]" and PNEUMA's "in which you live"; the Hebrew asher attem sham is literally "which you are there" -- "are" preserves the existential sense, while "live" is interpretive. (2) "blood" without article (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "the blood" (EMET) for haddam; while the Hebrew has the article, English "blood" in the generic/proverbial sense is more natural here. (3) "and no atonement can be made for the land" (LOGOS) is adopted over EMET's "and for the land no atonement can be made" (inverted word order) and PNEUMA's sentence break. LOGOS's order is clear while preserving the Hebrew content. (4) Single continuous sentence (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's two-sentence break.

---

### 35:34 -- "in which you [are] dwelling" vs. "in which you dwell"; "I [am] dwelling" vs. "I dwell" vs. "I am YHWH, dwelling"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And you shall not defile the land in which you [are] dwelling, in the midst of which I [am] dwelling, for I, YHWH, [am] dwelling in the midst of the sons of Israel." |
| LOGOS | "And you shall not defile the land in which you dwell, in the midst of which I dwell, for I, YHWH, dwell in the midst of the sons of Israel." |
| PNEUMA | "And you shall not defile the land in which you dwell, in the midst of which I dwell, for I am YHWH, dwelling in the midst of the sons of Israel." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And you shall not defile the land in which you dwell, in the midst of which I dwell, for I, YHWH, dwell in the midst of the sons of Israel." -- (1) "in which you dwell" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "in which you [are] dwelling" (EMET); the Hebrew yoshevim bah is a participle, and "dwell" in English captures the ongoing state without needing the periphrastic "are dwelling." (2) "I, YHWH, dwell" (LOGOS) is adopted over "I, YHWH, [am] dwelling" (EMET) and "I am YHWH, dwelling" (PNEUMA). EMET's periphrastic with brackets is unnecessary. PNEUMA restructures to "I am YHWH, dwelling" -- making "YHWH" a predicate nominative ("I am YHWH") rather than an appositive ("I, YHWH"), which changes the syntax. The Hebrew ani YHWH shokhen uses YHWH as an appositive to ani, with shokhen as the main participial verb: "I, YHWH, [am the one] dwelling." LOGOS's rendering preserves this structure most faithfully.
